common_name: Dorset Golden Apple (Ultra-Low-Chill Gulf Coast Apple)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile loam in full sun; Dorset Golden requires only 100–200 chill hours — it is one of very few apple cultivars that reliably produces fruit in zone 9a where chill hours frequently fall below 500 hours in warm winters.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Dorset Golden is one of the two or three apple cultivars with the lowest chill requirements that can be reliably grown in Gulf South zone 9 gardens — requiring only 100–200 chilling hours (hours below 45°F) for adequate dormancy break and fruit set. This ultra-low chill requirement makes Dorset Golden one of the very few apples that can be grown productively in the Gulf South where total winter chill hours range from 200–600 hours in zone 9, often falling short of the 700–1,000+ hours required by standard northern apple varieties. Dorset Golden originated as a chance seedling in the Bahamas — an unexpected confirmation that apples with very low chill requirements can produce high-quality fruit in tropical and subtropical climates. The fruit is medium to large, golden-yellow with a light blush, and has a firm, crisp texture with a good balance of sweetness and mild acidity — far superior in flavor to most other low-chill apple varieties. In zone 9, Dorset Golden ripens in June — exceptionally early for a full-sized apple. It is partially self-fertile but produces larger crops when cross-pollinated with Anna (another ultra-low-chill variety). For Gulf South apple growing, pair Dorset Golden with Anna for reliable cross-pollination.

common_name: Tropic Sweet Apple (Large Sweet Low-Chill Gulf South Apple)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Tropic Sweet requires approximately 150–200 chill hours and is best cross-pollinated with Dorsett Golden or Anna for maximum fruit set.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Tropic Sweet is a University of Florida low-chill apple introduction specifically developed for production in zone 8–10 gardens where chill hours are insufficient for standard northern apple cultivars. With only 150–200 chill hours required, Tropic Sweet is adapted to the winter climate of zone 9a where standard apple varieties cannot break dormancy properly. The fruits are large, round, yellow with a light blush, and distinguished by outstanding sweetness with very low acid content — a flavor profile that differs from the balanced sweet-tart quality of northern apples and appeals strongly to those who prefer sweet, mild apples. Tropic Sweet ripens in June in zone 9 — the June ripening window is well ahead of most summer fruit production and provides fresh orchard fruit at a time when few other tree fruits are ready. Like all low-chill apples, Tropic Sweet performs best when cross-pollinated with another low-chill cultivar such as Dorsett Golden or Anna. Proper dormant pruning (in late January–February) and fire blight management (preventive copper sprays during bloom) are the primary cultural practices for Gulf South apple production. The outstanding sweetness and large size make Tropic Sweet one of the finest fresh-eating apple choices for Gulf South gardeners willing to manage fire blight, which is the primary disease challenge in the humid Gulf South climate.

common_name: Honeynut Squash (Mini Intensely Sweet Butternut-Type Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer (July–August in zone 9) in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun for a fall harvest; Honeynut is a moschata type with better heat tolerance than pepo squash types, making it more reliable for late-season zone 9 production.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Honeynut is a landmark squash introduction from Cornell University and Row 7 Seed Company — a diminutive butternut-type squash (3–4 inches long, 6–8 oz) that is completely different from standard butternut squash in flavor intensity: the Honeynut's flesh has dramatically more concentrated sweetness, nuttiness, and butternut flavor than full-sized butternut because the ratio of flesh to seed cavity is much higher in the mini fruit. When roasted, the deep-orange flesh caramelizes beautifully and develops an intense, complex butterscotch-butternut flavor that has made Honeynut one of the most talked-about new vegetables of the 2010s since its public introduction in 2015. The small fruit size (one squash per serving) makes Honeynut practical for individual servings without the waste of a large standard butternut. As a Cucurbita moschata variety, Honeynut has better heat and disease tolerance (particularly vine borer resistance) than pepo-type winter squash — an important practical advantage in the Gulf South where squash vine borers and heat challenge pepo types in summer. In zone 9, direct sow in July–August for a fall harvest (September–November). Cure for 4–6 weeks after harvest to develop maximum sweetness.

common_name: Table Queen Acorn Squash (Classic Dark-Green Heirloom Acorn Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer (July–August in zone 9) in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun for a fall harvest; acorn squash can also be direct sown in March–April for a spring crop in zone 9, harvested before summer heat.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Table Queen is the classic American acorn squash — an heirloom variety that has defined the acorn squash category since its introduction in the 1890s and remains one of the most widely recognized winter squash forms in American cooking. The deeply ribbed, dark-green to nearly black fruits (1.5–2 lb, 4–6 inches long) have dense, golden-yellow, sweet flesh with a nutty, slightly dry texture that is outstanding for baking: cut in half, brush with butter, season with brown sugar and cinnamon, and bake until caramelized — the classic American acorn squash preparation. The deep ribs and dark color are distinctive enough that 'acorn squash' has become synonymous with Table Queen's specific shape. As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds can be saved from season to season. Table Queen stores well for 1–2 months at cool room temperature after proper curing (10 days at 80°F). In zone 9, acorn squash is most reliable as a fall crop (August planting, October–November harvest) — the spring planting often struggles in early summer heat. The fall crop benefits from the cooling temperatures of fall that improve the sweetness and flavor of the flesh. Space plants generously as the vines spread 6–8 feet in all directions.

common_name: Florida Beauty Strawberry (Day-Neutral Gulf Coast Strawberry)

planting.note: Transplant plugs or bare-root plants in fall (October–November in zone 9) in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile, sandy loam in full sun; Florida Beauty is a day-neutral variety developed for Gulf South production — it does not require the long cold periods needed by June-bearing northern varieties.

directions: Transplant plugs or bare-root plants in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, sandy loam in full sun; Florida Beauty is a University of Florida IFAS strawberry introduction developed specifically for Gulf Coast production — a day-neutral cultivar adapted to the warm winters and mild springs of zone 8–10 where standard June-bearing northern varieties (Chandler, Camarosa) perform unreliably in recent warmer winters. Day-neutral strawberries are not dependent on day length for flower initiation — they flower and fruit based on temperature rather than photoperiod, making them more reliably productive across a range of winter weather patterns in the Gulf South. Florida Beauty produces attractive, large (medium-to-large), bright red, excellent-flavored fruits with firm texture and good fresh-eating quality. In zone 9, transplant in October–November for a production window from January through April — the peak of Gulf South strawberry season when temperatures are ideal for fruit development and quality. The plants are treated as annuals in commercial production (removed after the spring harvest) but can be maintained as short-lived perennials in home garden beds. Florida Beauty represents the most recent generation of Gulf South strawberry breeding that addresses the challenges of warm-winter climate change on traditional day-length-sensitive cultivar performance.
sources: Gulf Coast Research and Education Center
